[11:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "AI Agents vs Regular Automations"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Comparação visual entre automação linear (if-then) e agentes AI com raciocínio não-linear
[11:15] So the main difference is like I'm sure like everyone here knows about make zap year and even go high level has like automation that you can do but the difference is agents like Jesse said is it uses more reasoning to like it picks what tools to use. It's not sequential. So regular automation and tools are like more linear like if then statements but like in for example which is what we're going to be talking about today mainly is more uh it uses reasoning.
[11:45] So like it can pick and choose what tools it needs to use to get to whatever thing that you're trying to do. So it's not necessarily linear. It just goes in order based off what it what tools it has access to and what it needs to do to get to the outcome.
[12:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Agent Process: Trigger → AI Agent → Tools → Outcome"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Diagrama de fluxo não-linear mostrando trigger node, AI agent com acesso a OpenAI/Claude, database e múltiplas ferramentas
[12:30] So typically the process of building an agent in a nonlinear way is you have some type of trigger node and I'll I'll I'll explain what that means, but basically you have something that starts it. So it could be a message, it can be an air table change, it could be a new type form or calendarly that comes in, it could um be a telegram message.
[13:00] Typically how I how I start my agents and I'm going to show you guys a real example of like simple agents and then like something that's really complex, but typically I start my agents through web hooks. Um, and if you're not familiar with web hook, it's basically like an action that happens and you send data into it.
[13:30] So for example, if you were in go high level and someone filled out a type form and go high level, you can actually add a web hook, send that type form information into that web hook and then pass that information into another application or software and then use that in that software.
[14:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "TOP Framework: Tasks, Operations, People"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Framework de três pilares para identificar oportunidades de automação com AI agents
[14:15] Um so then after that there's some other action. So um it'll visually looks like this. You have a trigger. You might have an AI agent here that is has access to open AI or has access to quad or any other AI model. It may have access to a database and then it may have access to multiple tools or just a singular tool and the agent is again in a non nonlinear way um building out whatever it needs to build out to get to the outcome that you are trying to do.
[14:45] So depending on your outcome, the agent is going to help get you there. Now the way I think about this is uh what I call the top framework. So when you're thinking about what what agents can help your business or like help you, you want to be thinking about what task operations or people can be can be replaced with AI agents.
[15:15] This is just a really easy way to remember this. So, as you're like as you're doing your day-to-day, always tell people to have like some type of running list of actions or processes or things that you do that you potentially could replace with AI or an AI agent or even an automation because sometimes you have to uh you have to choose if you want an AI agent to do the thing or you just want like a regular automation.

[16:00] So, when you're building these out, sometimes an agent is not the best choice for whatever you're trying to do. Maybe you need something more linear. Um, so you kind of have to pick and choose depending on your action, which I'll talk more about. But this is just a good way to think about like, okay, what task, what operations, what people can be replaced by agents.
[16:30] And by task, um, here's an example of an agent. So this is a very, very simple agent that, um, I built. So for my business, I run a a website called bestofi.com. And essentially, it's just a um a directory of different AI tools and news and stuff like that. So basically people can come to us they post their tool or product on our website and it gets published right.
[17:00] So for me I needed an agent to when a tool gets published on our end I needed to just basically send an email so I have some rules in here and we'll I'll show you what this looks like but basically I have like prompts in this agent that is has information about like how I want it to respond and things like things like that and then it sends an email.
[17:30] So as that web hook which was I talking about earlier. So like when they click publish it sends information into here. The agent gets that information and then it formulates the response and then sends the email. Um so like this is just an example of a very very simple agent that does a task for me.

[21:00] And it runs every single day for me where it's literally creating content on my behalf and my in my way of writing and all that and I don't really have to do much like I said besides approve or dis disapprove. Um, and the thing that I um I tell people as well is like the the way to think about this is always building stuff out before you actually build it out.
[21:30] So I use this tool called Excaladraw and it's basically a diagram, but like I basically just map out before I build out an agent or an automation or anything really. I just map out like how I think it should work. And these are all assumptions like as you build out your agent or automation. um this is not necessarily going to be the end result, but it is going to be a good place of like reference and like a baseline of like what you want to happen.

[25:00] So when someone asks a question, it's using rag to go find all like it's basically going and uh surfacing all of the data in the database from all the videos, all the transcriptions, all the telegram messages, all that stuff and then formulating a response using AI. So that in itself is an AI agent.

[28:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Agency Onboarding Agent Workflow"
NÚMEROS: 10-15 VSLs, 50-100 ads
VISUAL: Fluxograma completo mostrando: onboarding call → transcription → market research agent → VSSL agent → landing page agent → upsell/downsell email/ad agent → 50-100 ads gerados
[28:30] But I thought it was interesting how he built out his agent. He just kind of talked about the process. So he has some type of onboarding call that gets transcribed and sent into a market research agent. The market research agent does research on the target audience, core outcomes, limitations, emotions, consequences of not achieving the desired outcomes, excuses, objections, vision of the future and so on.
[29:00] And then the market research agent sends the completed doc to our VSSL agent that perfectly and um instantly executes 10 to 15 worldclass VSSL. So, and then he goes on to talk about more uh things that he does for like the onboarding stuff, right?
[29:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Modular Agent Components"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados

[29:45] Um, and I thought this was interesting just because I think it's relevant to everyone here, but also it proves a point where um when you're building out agents, you don't want one agent to do everything. Like, you need to have modular components to the overall outcome.
[30:15] So, remember how I said like you want to think kind of like reverse engineer like what you want your outcome to be. So like in this case he basically wants to be able to uh fulfill on his client duties right and it starts with a transcription and then it goes on. So like every part of this whole entire process has many processes within it.
[30:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Market Research Agent Sub-Tasks"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados

[31:00] And the first step to building any agent is again understanding what you're trying to accomplish which which means that you need to be thinking about what your outcome is first and then start to map out using a tool like I showed you excaladraw which is basically a diagram to get to that outcome.
[31:30] So, for example, what this um post said is like you're going to have an onboarding call, then it's going to do a market research agent, you're going to have a VSSL agent, you're going to have a landing page AI agent, and then you're going to have an upsell downell email advertising AI agent, and then that is going to complete 50 to 100 ads generated.
[32:00] So, like that's the entire process, but you can see that it's broken down into um different modular components of the entire process. And even within these different um agents, there's even more, right? So like really it's not it doesn't just stop at a market research agent, right?
[32:30] That market research agent has to go do research on the target audience. It then has to analyze the core outcomes. It has to identify limitations. It has to assess the emotions and so on. So like each part of um the agent process or the system has different parts of it to get to that ultimate outcome.

[1:26:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Using Structured Data in Next Agent"

VISUAL: Demonstração de como usar dados estruturados (ex: revenue range) como input para próximo agent na cadeia
[1:26:30] So for example, if I wanted to add a um another AI agent here, let's just say that this is the VSSL agent. DSL agent and let's say I wanted it to do um I should say like please find please find five um e-commerce brands that are and then let's just say that make this an expression and I say are in this range right.
[1:27:00] So I can pull in that revenue now and I can pull all of these individual components of the response so please find five e e-commerce brands that are um in between this range, right? Let's just say that I'm doing another research agent or something like that, right?
[1:27:30] Um, but the point is I can now have another agent that just uses uses individual components of that research that the AI agent did. So now I can say, okay, I'm just going to pull this to this one. You can actually I'm just not going I'm just going to pull that there.
[1:28:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Tool Reusability Between Agents"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Conceito de reutilizar ferramentas (Tavily Search) e modelos AI entre diferentes agents no workflow
[1:28:15] And um now I can pull this tool as well. You can reuse basically what I'm saying. You can reuse tools or or or AI models from other agents to new ones. And now if I say run this VSSL agent or do the research based off of what I said which is find five e-commerce brands that are in between this range which is going to be 100K to 2 million in revenue.
[1:28:45] Um it should go and take that in to consideration when it's doing its research for Tavi. So you can see here it's running again and now it should output five different brands um that are in that in that range.
[1:29:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Simple Agent Scalability"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Conceito de que agents simples podem escalar para complexidade ilimitada baseado em necessidades
[1:29:30] But this is how like this is like a very simple agent and you can take this as far as you want and you can also you know send this elsewhere. So like just say that this is the end of the agent, right? Like maybe you just wanted a market research agent. You didn't need the VSSL agent.
[1:30:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Sending Data to External Apps"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Opções de enviar output do agent para apps externos: Airtable, Google Sheets, Telegram, etc.
[1:30:15] Um maybe you wanted just the market research and you wanted to place that in air table for example. You can then add a um just another action, right? So you can literally say okay air table create a record right and now you can pull in the data from these past nodes.
[1:30:45] So like from the VSSO agent, AI agent um or any other the past nodes and actually add data. So if it's Air Table, Google, Telegram, whatever, you can basically just send that data wherever you want. Um, just like how you would do in a Zap year or make.
[1:31:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "VSSL Agent Output"
NÚMEROS: 5 e-commerce brands, $100K-$2M revenue
VISUAL: Output do segundo agent mostrando lista de 5 marcas e-commerce dentro do range de receita especificado
[1:31:30] Um, so now the VSSL agent, which is really not a VSSL agent, it's a market research part two agent. But the point is it did the research, right? So here are five e-commerce brands that fall within 100K to $2 million or in revenue range, right?
[1:32:00] So um, and then it just gave me different brands, right? And then now I can use that for whatever and I can take this to, like I said, any other app.

Or again like web hooks, you can also do human in the loop elements, which is essentially having a human approve or decline a particular action for whenever the AI agent is done.

[1:35:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "When to Use Structured Output"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Explicação de quando usar structured output: quando precisa acessar pontos de dados específicos em partes subsequentes do processo
[1:36:00] Gelani: Great question. So, it really comes down to the data that you need. So, for me, like for example, like this long long ass response beforehand, um, let me move this out of the way. So like for example like the message right like this is like you can't really dissect things out of this to use in other parts of the process.
[1:36:30] So you want to basically structure the output. So structure what the AI agent's response is into better um better usability for other parts of the process. You don't always have to do a structured output. It just depends on if you need access to data points that are inside of um that response.
[1:37:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Content Selection Agent Example"
NÚMEROS: 10 insights
VISUAL: Exemplo real de uso: agent que encontra 10 insights, cada um precisa estar separado (não em texto único) com index, insight e data
[1:37:15] So sometimes like um like a real example of this is uh let me exit out of here. So like a real example here like in in a production use case is um a content selection AI agent. So like this AI agent, the goal is for it to find different insights for me, like 10 different insights.
[1:37:45] And I need those 10 different insights to not be in one like mega long wall of text. I need them to be split out in in individual insights. So in this structured output, I'm basically saying like I need you to break out each index or each insight in this manner.
[1:38:15] So like I want to index, I want the insight that it's broken down to and the date of that insight that it came from and I want you to continue on for as how many insights that you provide me. So it's not necessarily like one long text. It's now broken into um different insights from that long text if that makes sense.
[1:38:45] So it just depends on um like your use case and like what you're trying to achieve for each part of the process.
Participant: If you go too long, is it not going to work
[1:39:00] Gelani: Uh for sorry like what do you mean
Participant: Like if you don't break it down uh like you or reduce it down like you did? Is it is the bot not going to work?
[1:39:15] Gelani: No, it'll work. It just it'll it just it's just one long text instead of something that's broken into parts that you need. So yeah, it's it's totally up to like what you're trying to do. Sometimes I don't use this just because I don't need it to be broken into parts. I just I just need the text to come back, right?
[1:39:45] And then I need to use that long text into the other parts of the process. It just totally depends on what you're trying to do. Does that answer your question?
Participant: Yeah. Cool. Thank you.
Gelani: Okay, awesome.

[1:45:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "GoHighLevel vs N8N Decision"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Critérios de decisão: quantidade de variáveis/contexto necessário, padronização vs personalização, integração entre plataformas
[1:45:30] Gelani: Yeah. Um, it depends on if the information that you're providing is like how how are there a lot of variables to the information that's being provided? And the reason why I say that is because an agent, if you build an AI agent, you can provide it a lot of context and a lot of additional information that it can pull from to formulate the responses.
[1:46:00] Versus if you had something that's more standardized and it's just the same thing for every single person, then you might just want to build it and go high level. Um, but it comes down to that, I would say.

📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
$2.1 milhões adicionados à agência em 6 meses usando AI agents
80% de automação de tarefas mencionada como objetivo
10-15 VSLs de classe mundial gerados automaticamente
50-100 anúncios gerados por processo
20-30+ vídeos de aprendizado recomendados
Range de receita exemplo: $100K-$2M
10 insights de conteúdo por execução
12 meses estimados até agents construírem agents automaticamente
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
TOP Framework: Tasks, Operations, People - framework para identificar oportunidades de substituição por AI agents
Modular Agent Architecture: Quebrar processos complexos em múltiplos agents especializados
RAG System: Retrieval Augmented Generation para busca precisa em databases (usado no Jeremy AI)
Structured Output Parser: Transformar respostas longas em JSON estruturado para reutilização
Human-in-the-Loop: Sistema de aprovação humana em pontos críticos do workflow
Webhook Triggers: Método preferido para iniciar workflows de agents
No-Code First Approach: Priorizar soluções no-code antes de partir para código
ESTRUTURA:
Fluxo Básico de Agent: Trigger → AI Agent (com acesso a Model + Tools + Memory) → Actions → Output
Processo de Onboarding Automatizado: Call → Transcription → Market Research Agent → VSSL Agent → Landing Page Agent → Ads Agent
Market Research Agent Sub-tasks: Target audience research → Core outcomes analysis → Limitations identification → Emotions assessment → Consequences → Objections → Vision mapping
Ferramentas Recomendadas:
Construção: N8N (no-code), Replit, Bolt.new, Lovable.dev
Pesquisa: Perplexity, Tavily Search
APIs: Rapid API, Apify (marketplaces)
Planejamento: Excalidraw (diagramação)
Modelos: OpenAI, Anthropic Claude, Open Router (acesso a todos)
Integrações: GoHighLevel, Airtable, HubSpot, Stripe, PayPal, Telegram, LinkedIn, X (Twitter)
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
N8N: Fairly cheap, opção self-hosted com automações ilimitadas
OpenAI API: $20-$50 créditos iniciais recomendados para testes
Investimento não divulgado da Jeremy em múltiplos softwares construídos por Gelani:
Hammer Them software
Telegram summary bot
Financial modeling tools
Confirmation page video generator (em desenvolvimento)
PRINCÍPIOS-CHAVE:
Sempre planejar antes de construir usando ferramentas de diagramação
Agents não são lineares - usam raciocínio para escolher ferramentas
Um agent não deve fazer tudo - modularidade é essencial
Structured output quando precisar reutilizar dados específicos
Testar extensivamente prompts, tools e outputs
Documentação é sua amiga - sempre consultar docs de APIs
IA pode ajudar a encontrar ferramentas - pergunte ao ChatGPT/Claude
"IMPORTANT:" em prompts aumenta taxa de compliance para 9/10
DURAÇÃO: ~2 horas FORMATO: Masterclass ao vivo com demonstração técnica, Q&A interativo, build em tempo real
